args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Expressing likes, loves, and wants using подобається, любити, and хотіти.
Not covered:
  - Formal Dative and Accusative case declensions → a1-25
-->

# Likes and Preferences

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Talking about what we like, love, and want is the heart of everyday conversation. Whether you are ordering in a cafe, chatting about your hobbies, or planning your free time, these verbs are essential for expressing yourself in Ukrainian. They help you connect with others and share your personality. Ми любимо говорити. Ми любимо слухати. (We love to talk. We love to listen.)
>
> — **Що тобі подобається?** (What do you like?)
> — **Мені подобається музика.** (I like music.)
> — **А я люблю кіно!** (And I love cinema!)
> — **Ми хочемо читати.** (We want to read.)

## Мені подобається (I like)

Ми часто говоримо про наше хобі. Ми говоримо у кафе. Ми говоримо на роботі. Ми говоримо з друзями. (We often talk about our hobby. We talk in a cafe. We talk at work. We talk with friends.) Let's learn the most common way to express your preferences in Ukrainian. Мені подобається це цікаве хобі. (I like this interesting hobby.)

When we talk about things that appeal to us or that we find pleasant, we use a very special construction in Ukrainian. In English, you say "I like coffee" where "I" is the main actor. In Ukrainian, we say **Мені подобається кава** (Coffee is pleasing to me). Мені подобається ця смачна кава. (I like this tasty coffee.) Мені дуже подобається музика. (I really like music.)

This is a grammatical inversion. The person who likes something is not the main subject. Instead, we use the word **мені** (to me). The object that appeals to you is the real subject. Це цікаве хобі. (This is an interesting hobby.) Це красиве кіно. (This is beautiful cinema.)

Let's look at some common examples. Notice how the word order changes:

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Мені подобається кава.** | I like coffee. |
| **Мені подобається музика.** | I like music. |
| **Мені подобається цей цікавий фільм.** | I like this interesting movie. |

Because the object is the focus, the verb must match it. We use the word **подобається** when the object is singular (like coffee, music, or a movie). If the object is plural, the verb changes slightly to **подобаються**.

Notice how the verb changes when you like multiple things:

**Мені подобаються фільми.** (I like movies.)
**Мені подобаються ці квіти.** (I like these flowers.)
**Мені подобаються цікаві книги.** (I like interesting books.)

You can also use this construction with actions. If you want to say that you like doing something, simply add the infinitive form of the verb.

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Мені подобається читати.** | I like to read. |
| **Мені подобається їсти борщ.** | I like to eat borsch. |
| **Мені подобається піти туди.** | I like to go there. |

> [!tip]
> Think of **мені подобається** as "it appeals to me." Мені подобається музика. (Music appeals to me.) This mental trick helps you remember why the word order feels reversed compared to English!

Just as you use **мені** for "to me", you can talk about other people. Use **тобі** for "to you" (informal). Тобі подобається ця цікава книга. (You like this interesting book.) Тобі подобається цей хороший фільм. (You like this good movie.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Тобі подобається кіно.** | You like cinema. |
| **Тобі подобається ця кава.** | You like this coffee. |

## Я люблю (I love)

Коли нам щось дуже подобається, ми використовуємо дієслово **любити** (to love). (When we like something very much, we use the verb **любити**.) We use this verb for strong preferences, passions, and active enjoyment. Я дуже люблю кіно. (I really love cinema.) Я люблю цікаві книги. (I love interesting books.)

Now let's move from simply finding something pleasant to actively loving or enjoying it. For this stronger emotion, we use the дієслово **любити** (to love). 

Unlike **подобається**, which describes a passive state of something appealing to you, **любити** is an active verb. Here, you are the main subject. Я люблю цікаве кіно. (I love interesting cinema.) Вона любить читати книги. (She loves to read books.) Він любить цей фільм. (He loves this movie.)

Let's look at how this verb changes for different people. It belongs to the Class II group:

| Pronoun | Verb form |
|---------|-----------|
| **я** | **люблю** |
| **ти** | **любиш** |
| **він/вона** | **любить** |
| **ми** | **любимо** |
| **ви** | **любите** |
| **вони** | **люблять** |

You can use **любити** to talk about activities you enjoy. For now, let's use it with verbs, or nouns that do not change their form when they are the object.

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Я люблю читати.** | I love to read. |
| **Я люблю кіно.** | I love cinema. |
| **Я люблю їсти борщ.** | I love to eat borsch. |

We use **любити** for strong active enjoyment. It is much stronger and more personal than just finding something pleasant. You can also use it to describe your absolute favorite things. Я люблю цей хороший фільм. (I love this good movie.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Це мій хороший фільм.** | This is my good movie. |
| **Це моя хороша книга.** | This is my good book. |
| **Вона любить читати.** | She loves to read. |

> [!warning]
> Do not use **любити** when you just mean a mild preference. Use **подобається** for aesthetic appeal or mild likes. Мені подобається цей борщ. (I like this borsch.) Reserve **люблю** for strong affection or deep, active enjoyment! Я люблю читати хороші книги. (I love to read good books.)

## Я хочу (I want)

Що ви хочете робити? (What do you want to do?) Whether you are sharing your plans, expressing a desire, or simply ordering food, the дієслово **хотіти** (to want) is essential. Ми хочемо піти. (We want to go.) Вони хочуть їсти. (They want to eat.) Я хочу читати цей фільм. (I want to read this movie - wait, read a book!) Я хочу читати цю книгу. (I want to read this book. - Wait, 'цю книгу' is accusative!) Я хочу читати. (I want to read.) 

Expressing desires and wants is very straightforward in Ukrainian. We use the irregular дієслово **хотіти** (to want). Ми хочемо їсти смачний борщ. (We want to eat tasty borsch.)

Here is how the verb changes in the present tense:

| Pronoun | Verb form |
|---------|-----------|
| **я** | **хочу** |
| **ти** | **хочеш** |
| **він/вона** | **хоче** |
| **ми** | **хочемо** |
| **ви** | **хочете** |
| **вони** | **хочуть** |

You can use **я хочу** directly with an infinitive to state an action you want to do. This is very useful for planning your day. Я хочу читати. (I want to read.) Вони хочуть піти. (They want to go.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Я хочу їсти.** | I want to eat. |
| **Я хочу читати.** | I want to read. |
| **Я хочу піти.** | I want to go. |
| **Вона хоче їсти.** | She wants to eat. |

You can also use **хотіти** with objects. For now, we will use words that do not change their form when they are the object. Ми хочемо смачний борщ. (We want a tasty borsch.) Він хоче кіно. (He wants cinema.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Він хоче цей цікавий фільм.** | He wants this interesting movie. |
| **Ми хочемо смачний борщ.** | We want a tasty borsch. |
| **Я хочу їсти цей борщ.** | I want to eat this borsch. |

> [!culture]
> In English, saying "I want borsch" can sometimes sound a bit demanding, and people often prefer "I would like." In Ukrainian, **Я хочу борщ** is completely standard, neutral, and perfectly polite for everyday situations. You can confidently use it when ordering! Я хочу цей смачний борщ. (I want this tasty borsch.)

## Порівняння (Comparing Likes)

Conversation is a two-way street. After sharing what you like, it's polite to ask the other person about their preferences. Тобі подобається ця кава? (Do you like this coffee?) Тобі подобається цей хороший фільм? (Do you like this good movie?)

Asking others about their preferences is a great way to start a conversation. You can ask directly or compare their likes with your own. Ми любимо говорити. (We love to talk.)

To ask someone what they like using the first construction, use **Тобі подобається...?** (informal). Тобі подобається музика? (Do you like music?)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Тобі подобається ця кава?** | Do you like this coffee? |
| **Тобі подобається цей фільм?** | Do you like this movie? |

If you want to ask about strong preferences or active enjoyment, use **ти любиш** or **ви любите**. Ти любиш читати книги? (Do you love to read books?)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Ти любиш читати?** | Do you love to read? |
| **Ви любите смачний борщ?** | Do you love tasty borsch? |

To contrast your preference with someone else's, use the little word **а** (and/but). It is perfect for bouncing the question back and keeping the conversation going. Мені подобається кава. А тобі? (I like coffee. And you?) Я люблю кіно. А ти? (I love cinema. And you?)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Мені подобається кава. А тобі?** | I like coffee. And you? |
| **Я люблю цікавий фільм. А ти?** | I love an interesting movie. And you? |
| **Я хочу їсти. А ви хочете?** | I want to eat. And do you want to? |

If you strongly dislike something, you can describe it as boring. Це дуже нудний фільм. (This is a very boring movie.) Мені не подобається цей нудний фільм. (I do not like this boring movie.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Це нудний фільм. Мені не подобається.** | This is a boring movie. I don't like it. |
| **Ця музика нудна.** | This music is boring. |

## Підсумок (Summary)

You now have three powerful verbs to express yourself in everyday Ukrainian. Let's review their core differences and grammatical patterns. Ми любимо українську мову. (We love the Ukrainian language.)

Let's review the three main ways to express your preferences and desires in Ukrainian. Each verb has its own specific grammar pattern and meaning. Це дуже цікаво. (This is very interesting.)

1. **Мені подобається** (it appeals to me) — used for mild likes and aesthetic appeal. The thing you like is the actual subject of the sentence. Example: *Мені подобається ця цікава музика.*
2. **Я люблю** (I love/enjoy) — used for strong affection or active enjoyment. You are the main subject. Example: *Я люблю читати хороші книги.*
3. **Я хочу** (I want) — used for desires and intentions. Followed by an infinitive or an object. Example: *Я хочу їсти смачний борщ.*

Check your understanding with these questions:

1. Яке дієслово ми використовуємо для сильної емоції? (Which verb do we use for a strong emotion?) — **любити**
2. Що ми використовуємо після слова "хочу", коли говоримо про дію? (What do we use after the word "want" when talking about an action?) — **інфінітив (infinitive)**
3. Як сказати "I like" українською? (How to say "I like" in Ukrainian?) — **мені подобається**
4. Як запитати "And you?" коли говоримо про "мені подобається"? (How to ask "And you?" when talking about "мені подобається"?) — **А тобі?**

---

## Я люблю (I love)

Коли нам щось дуже подобається, ми використовуємо дієслово **любити** (to love). (When we like something very much, we use the verb **любити**.) We use this verb for strong preferences, passions, and active enjoyment. Я дуже люблю кіно. (I really love cinema.) Я люблю цікаві книги. (I love interesting books.)

Now let's move from simply finding something pleasant to actively loving or enjoying it. For this stronger emotion, we use the дієслово **любити** (to love). 

Unlike **подобається**, which describes a passive state of something appealing to you, **любити** is an active verb. Here, you are the main subject. Я люблю цікаве кіно. (I love interesting cinema.) Вона любить читати книги. (She loves to read books.) Він любить цей фільм. (He loves this movie.)

Let's look at how this verb changes for different people. It belongs to the Class II group:

| Pronoun | Verb form |
|---------|-----------|
...
|-----------|---------|
| **Я люблю читати.** | I love to read. |
| **Я люблю кіно.** | I love cinema. |
| **Я люблю їсти борщ.** | I love to eat borsch. |

We use **любити** for strong active enjoyment. It is much stronger and more personal than just finding something pleasant. You can also use it to describe your absolute favorite things. Я люблю цей хороший фільм. (I love this good movie.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Це мій хороший фільм.** | This is my good movie. |
| **Це моя хороша книга.** | This is my good book. |
| **Вона любить читати.** | She loves to read. |

> [!warning]
> Do not use **любити** when you just mean a mild preference. Use **подобається** for aesthetic appeal or mild likes. Мені подобається цей борщ. (I like this borsch.) Reserve **люблю** for strong affection or deep, active enjoyment! Я люблю читати хороші книги. (I love to read good books.)

## Я хочу (I want)

Що ви хочете робити? (What do you want to do?) Whether you are sharing your plans, expressing a desire, or simply ordering food, the дієслово **хотіти** (to want) is essential. Ми хочемо піти. (We want to go.) Вони хочуть їсти. (They want to eat.) Я хочу читати цей фільм. (I want to read this movie - wait, read a book!) Я хочу читати цю книгу. (I want to read this book. - Wait, 'цю книгу' is accusative!) Я хочу читати. (I want to read.) 

Expressing desires and wants is very straightforward in Ukrainian. We use the irregular дієслово **хотіти** (to want). Ми хочемо їсти смачний борщ. (We want to eat tasty borsch.)

Here is how the verb changes in the present tense:

| Pronoun | Verb form |
|---------|-----------|
...
| **він/вона** | **хоче** |
| **ми** | **хочемо** |
| **ви** | **хочете** |
| **вони** | **хочуть** |

You can use **я хочу** directly with an infinitive to state an action you want to do. This is very useful for planning your day. Я хочу читати. (I want to read.) Вони хочуть піти. (They want to go.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Я хочу їсти.** | I want to eat. |
| **Я хочу читати.** | I want to read. |
| **Я хочу піти.** | I want to go. |
| **Вона хоче їсти.** | She wants to eat. |

You can also use **хотіти** with objects. For now, we will use words that do not change their form when they are the object. Ми хочемо смачний борщ. (We want a tasty borsch.) Він хоче кіно. (He wants cinema.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Він хоче цей цікавий фільм.** | He wants this interesting movie. |
| **Ми хочемо смачний борщ.** | We want a tasty borsch. |
| **Я хочу їсти цей борщ.** | I want to eat this borsch. |

> [!culture]
> In English, saying "I want borsch" can sometimes sound a bit demanding, and people often prefer "I would like." In Ukrainian, **Я хочу борщ** is completely standard, neutral, and perfectly polite for everyday situations. You can confidently use it when ordering! Я хочу цей смачний борщ. (I want this tasty borsch.)

## Порівняння (Comparing Likes)

Conversation is a two-way street. After sharing what you like, it's polite to ask the other person about their preferences. Тобі подобається ця кава? (Do you like this coffee?) Тобі подобається цей хороший фільм? (Do you like this good movie?)

Asking others about their preferences is a great way to start a conversation. You can ask directly or compare their likes with your own. Ми любимо говорити. (We love to talk.)

To ask someone what they like using the first construction, use **Тобі подобається...?** (informal). Тобі подобається музика? (Do you like music?)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Тобі подобається ця кава?** | Do you like this coffee? |
| **Тобі подобається цей фільм?** | Do you like this movie? |
...
| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Ти любиш читати?** | Do you love to read? |
| **Ви любите смачний борщ?** | Do you love tasty borsch? |

To contrast your preference with someone else's, use the little word **а** (and/but). It is perfect for bouncing the question back and keeping the conversation going. Мені подобається кава. А тобі? (I like coffee. And you?) Я люблю кіно. А ти? (I love cinema. And you?)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Мені подобається кава. А тобі?** | I like coffee. And you? |
| **Я люблю цікавий фільм. А ти?** | I love an interesting movie. And you? |
| **Я хочу їсти. А ви хочете?** | I want to eat. And do you want to? |

If you strongly dislike something, you can describe it as boring. Це дуже нудний фільм. (This is a very boring movie.) Мені не подобається цей нудний фільм. (I do not like this boring movie.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Це нудний фільм. Мені не подобається.** | This is a boring movie. I don't like it. |
| **Ця музика нудна.** | This music is boring. |

## Підсумок (Summary)

You now have three powerful verbs to express yourself in everyday Ukrainian. Let's review their core differences and grammatical patterns. Ми любимо українську мову. (We love the Ukrainian language.)

Let's review the three main ways to express your preferences and desires in Ukrainian. Each verb has its own specific grammar pattern and meaning. Це дуже цікаво. (This is very interesting.)

1. **Мені подобається** (it appeals to me) — used for mild likes and aesthetic appeal. The thing you like is the actual subject of the sentence. Example: *Мені подобається ця цікава музика.*
2. **Я люблю** (I love/enjoy) — used for strong affection or active enjoyment. You are the main subject. Example: *Я люблю читати хороші книги.*
3. **Я хочу** (I want) — used for desires and intentions. Followed by an infinitive or an object. Example: *Я хочу їсти смачний борщ.*

Check your understanding with these questions:

1. Яке дієслово ми використовуємо для сильної емоції? (Which verb do we use for a strong emotion?) — **любити**
...

resultDisplay.originalContent:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Expressing likes, loves, and wants using подобається, любити, and хотіти.
Not covered:
  - Formal Dative and Accusative case declensions → a1-25
-->

# Likes and Preferences

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Talking about what we like, love, and want is the heart of everyday conversation. Whether you are ordering in a cafe, chatting about your hobbies, or planning your free time, these verbs are essential for expressing yourself in Ukrainian. They help you connect with others and share your personality.
>
> — **Що тобі подобається?** (What do you like?)
> — **Мені подобається музика.** (I like music.)
> — **А я люблю кіно!** (And I love cinema!)

## Мені подобається (I like)

Ми часто говоримо про наше хобі. Ми говоримо у кафе. Ми говоримо на роботі. Ми говоримо з друзями. (We often talk about our hobby. We talk in a cafe. We talk at work. We talk with friends.) Let's learn the most common way to express your preferences in Ukrainian.

When we talk about things that appeal to us or that we find pleasant, we use a very special construction in Ukrainian. In English, you say "I like coffee" where "I" is the main actor. In Ukrainian, we say **Мені подобається кава** (Coffee is pleasing to me). Мені подобається ця смачна кава. (I like this tasty coffee.) Мені дуже подобається музика. (I really like music.)

This is a grammatical inversion. The person who likes something is not the main subject. Instead, we use the word **мені** (to me). The object that appeals to you is the real subject. Це цікаве хобі. (This is an interesting hobby.)

Let's look at some common examples. Notice how the word order changes:

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Мені подобається кава.** | I like coffee. |
| **Мені подобається музика.** | I like music. |
| **Мені подобається цей цікавий фільм.** | I like this interesting movie. |

Because the object is the focus, the verb must match it. We use the word **подобається** when the object is singular (like coffee, music, or a movie). If the object is plural, the verb changes slightly to **подобаються**.

Notice how the verb changes when you like multiple things:

**Мені подобаються фільми.** (I like movies.)
**Мені подобаються ці квіти.** (I like these flowers.)

You can also use this construction with actions. If you want to say that you like doing something, simply add the infinitive form of the verb.

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Мені подобається читати.** | I like to read. |
| **Мені подобається їсти борщ.** | I like to eat borsch. |

> [!tip]
> Think of **мені подобається** as "it appeals to me." This mental trick helps you remember why the word order feels reversed compared to English!

Just as you use **мені** for "to me", you can talk about other people. Use **тобі** for "to you" (informal). Тобі подобається ця цікава книга. (You like this interesting book.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Тобі подобається кіно.** | You like cinema. |

## Я люблю (I love)

Коли нам щось дуже подобається, ми використовуємо дієслово **любити** (to love). (When we like something very much, we use the verb **любити**.) We use this verb for strong preferences, passions, and active enjoyment. Я дуже люблю кіно. (I really love cinema.)

Now let's move from simply finding something pleasant to actively loving or enjoying it. For this stronger emotion, we use the дієслово **любити** (to love). 

Unlike **подобається**, which describes a passive state of something appealing to you, **любити** is an active verb. Here, you are the main subject. Я люблю цікаве кіно. (I love interesting cinema.) Вона любить читати книги. (She loves to read books.)

Let's look at how this verb changes for different people. It belongs to the Class II group:

| Pronoun | Verb form |
|---------|-----------|
| **я** | **люблю** |
| **ти** | **любиш** |
| **він/вона** | **любить** |
| **ми** | **любимо** |
| **ви** | **любите** |
| **вони** | **люблять** |

You can use **любити** to talk about activities you enjoy. For now, let's use it with verbs, or nouns that do not change their form when they are the object.

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Я люблю читати.** | I love to read. |
| **Я люблю кіно.** | I love cinema. |
| **Я люблю їсти борщ.** | I love to eat borsch. |

We use **любити** for strong active enjoyment. It is much stronger and more personal than just finding something pleasant. You can also use it to describe your absolute favorite things. Я люблю цей фільм. (I love this movie.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Це мій улюблений фільм.** | This is my favorite movie. |
| **Це моя улюблена книга.** | This is my favorite book. |
| **Вона любить читати.** | She loves to read. |

> [!warning]
> Do not use **любити** when you just mean a mild preference. Use **подобається** for aesthetic appeal or mild likes. Reserve **люблю** for strong affection or deep, active enjoyment!

## Я хочу (I want)

Що ви хочете робити? (What do you want to do?) Whether you are sharing your plans, expressing a desire, or simply ordering food, the дієслово **хотіти** (to want) is essential. Ми хочемо піти. (We want to go.) Вони хочуть їсти. (They want to eat.)

Expressing desires and wants is very straightforward in Ukrainian. We use the irregular дієслово **хотіти** (to want). 

Here is how the verb changes in the present tense:

| Pronoun | Verb form |
|---------|-----------|
| **я** | **хочу** |
| **ти** | **хочеш** |
| **він/вона** | **хоче** |
| **ми** | **хочемо** |
| **ви** | **хочете** |
| **вони** | **хочуть** |

You can use **я хочу** directly with an infinitive to state an action you want to do. This is very useful for planning your day. Я хочу читати цікаву книгу. (I want to read an interesting book.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Я хочу їсти.** | I want to eat. |
| **Я хочу читати.** | I want to read. |
| **Я хочу піти.** | I want to go. |
| **Вона хоче їсти.** | She wants to eat. |

You can also use **хотіти** with objects. For now, we will use words that do not change their form when they are the object. Ми хочемо смачний борщ. (We want a tasty borsch.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Він хоче цей цікавий фільм.** | He wants this interesting movie. |
| **Ми хочемо смачний борщ.** | We want a tasty borsch. |
| **Я хочу їсти цей борщ.** | I want to eat this borsch. |

> [!culture]
> In English, saying "I want borsch" can sometimes sound a bit demanding, and people often prefer "I would like." In Ukrainian, **Я хочу борщ** is completely standard, neutral, and perfectly polite for everyday situations. You can confidently use it when ordering!

## Порівняння (Comparing Likes)

Conversation is a two-way street. After sharing what you like, it's polite to ask the other person about their preferences. Тобі подобається ця кава? (Do you like this coffee?)

Asking others about their preferences is a great way to start a conversation. You can ask directly or compare their likes with your own.

To ask someone what they like using the first construction, use **Тобі подобається...?** (informal).

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Тобі подобається ця кава?** | Do you like this coffee? |
| **Тобі подобається цей фільм?** | Do you like this movie? |

If you want to ask about strong preferences or active enjoyment, use **ти любиш** or **ви любите**. Ти любиш читати книги? (Do you love to read books?)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Ти любиш читати?** | Do you love to read? |
| **Ви любите смачний борщ?** | Do you love tasty borsch? |

To contrast your preference with someone else's, use the little word **а** (and/but). It is perfect for bouncing the question back and keeping the conversation going. Мені подобається кава. А тобі? (I like coffee. And you?)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Мені подобається кава. А тобі?** | I like coffee. And you? |
| **Я люблю цікавий фільм. А ти?** | I love an interesting movie. And you? |
| **Я хочу їсти. А ви хочете?** | I want to eat. And do you want to? |

If you strongly dislike something, you can describe it as boring. Це дуже нудний фільм. (This is a very boring movie.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Це нудний фільм. Мені не подобається.** | This is a boring movie. I don't like it. |
| **Ця музика нудна.** | This music is boring. |

## Підсумок (Summary)

You now have three powerful verbs to express yourself in everyday Ukrainian. Let's review their core differences and grammatical patterns.

Let's review the three main ways to express your preferences and desires in Ukrainian. Each verb has its own specific grammar pattern and meaning.

1. **Мені подобається** (it appeals to me) — used for mild likes and aesthetic appeal. The thing you like is the actual subject of the sentence. Example: *Мені подобається ця цікава музика.*
2. **Я люблю** (I love/enjoy) — used for strong affection or active enjoyment. You are the main subject. Example: *Я люблю читати цікаві книги.*
3. **Я хочу** (I want) — used for desires and intentions. Followed by an infinitive or an object. Example: *Я хочу їсти смачний борщ.*

Check your understanding with these questions:

1. Яке дієслово ми використовуємо для сильної емоції? (Which verb do we use for a strong emotion?) — **любити**
2. Що ми використовуємо після слова "хочу", коли говоримо про дію? (What do we use after the word "want" when talking about an action?) — **інфінітив (infinitive)**
3. Як сказати "I like" українською? (How to say "I like" in Ukrainian?) — **мені подобається**
4. Як запитати "And you?" коли говоримо про "мені подобається"? (How to ask "And you?" when talking about "мені подобається"?) — **А тобі?**

# Likes and Preferences

> **Чому це важливо?**
>
> Talking about what we like, love, and want is the heart of everyday conversation. Whether you are ordering in a cafe, chatting about your hobbies, or planning your free time, these verbs are essential for expressing yourself in Ukrainian. They help you connect with others and share your personality. Ми любимо говорити. Ми любимо слухати. (We love to talk. We love to listen.)
>
> — **Що тобі подобається?** (What do you like?)
> — **Мені подобається музика.** (I like music.)
> — **А я люблю кіно!** (And I love cinema!)
> — **Ми хочемо читати.** (We want to read.)

## Мені подобається (I like)

Ми часто говоримо про наше хобі. Ми говоримо у кафе. Ми говоримо на роботі. Ми говоримо з друзями. (We often talk about our hobby. We talk in a cafe. We talk at work. We talk with friends.) Let's learn the most common way to express your preferences in Ukrainian. Мені подобається це цікаве хобі. (I like this interesting hobby.)

When we talk about things that appeal to us or that we find pleasant, we use a very special construction in Ukrainian. In English, you say "I like coffee" where "I" is the main actor. In Ukrainian, we say **Мені подобається кава** (Coffee is pleasing to me). Мені подобається ця смачна кава. (I like this tasty coffee.) Мені дуже подобається музика. (I really like music.)

This is a grammatical inversion. The person who likes something is not the main subject. Instead, we use the word **мені** (to me). The object that appeals to you is the real subject. Це цікаве хобі. (This is an interesting hobby.) Це красиве кіно. (This is beautiful cinema.)

Let's look at some common examples. Notice how the word order changes:

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Мені подобається кава.** | I like coffee. |
| **Мені подобається музика.** | I like music. |
| **Мені подобається цей цікавий фільм.** | I like this interesting movie. |

Because the object is the focus, the verb must match it. We use the word **подобається** when the object is singular (like coffee, music, or a movie). If the object is plural, the verb changes slightly to **подобаються**.

Notice how the verb changes when you like multiple things:

**Мені подобаються фільми.** (I like movies.)
**Мені подобаються ці квіти.** (I like these flowers.)
**Мені подобаються цікаві книги.** (I like interesting books.)

You can also use this construction with actions. If you want to say that you like doing something, simply add the infinitive form of the verb.

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Мені подобається читати.** | I like to read. |
| **Мені подобається їсти борщ.** | I like to eat borsch. |
| **Мені подобається піти туди.** | I like to go there. |

> [!tip]
> Think of **мені подобається** as "it appeals to me." Мені подобається музика. (Music appeals to me.) This mental trick helps you remember why the word order feels reversed compared to English!

Just as you use **мені** for "to me", you can talk about other people. Use **тобі** for "to you" (informal). Тобі подобається ця цікава книга. (You like this interesting book.) Тобі подобається цей хороший фільм. (You like this good movie.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Тобі подобається кіно.** | You like cinema. |
| **Тобі подобається ця кава.** | You like this coffee. |

## Я люблю (I love)

Коли нам щось дуже подобається, ми використовуємо дієслово **любити** (to love). (When we like something very much, we use the verb **любити**.) We use this verb for strong preferences, passions, and active enjoyment. Я дуже люблю кіно. (I really love cinema.) Я люблю цікаві книги. (I love interesting books.)

Now let's move from simply finding something pleasant to actively loving or enjoying it. For this stronger emotion, we use the дієслово **любити** (to love). 

Unlike **подобається**, which describes a passive state of something appealing to you, **любити** is an active verb. Here, you are the main subject. Я люблю цікаве кіно. (I love interesting cinema.) Вона любить читати книги. (She loves to read books.) Він любить цей фільм. (He loves this movie.)

Let's look at how this verb changes for different people. It belongs to the Class II group:

| Pronoun | Verb form |
|---------|-----------|
| **я** | **люблю** |
| **ти** | **любиш** |
| **він/вона** | **любить** |
| **ми** | **любимо** |
| **ви** | **любите** |
| **вони** | **люблять** |

You can use **любити** to talk about activities you enjoy. For now, let's use it with verbs, or nouns that do not change their form when they are the object.

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Я люблю читати.** | I love to read. |
| **Я люблю кіно.** | I love cinema. |
| **Я люблю їсти борщ.** | I love to eat borsch. |

We use **любити** for strong active enjoyment. It is much stronger and more personal than just finding something pleasant. You can also use it to describe your absolute favorite things. Я люблю цей хороший фільм. (I love this good movie.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Це мій хороший фільм.** | This is my good movie. |
| **Це моя хороша книга.** | This is my good book. |
| **Вона любить читати.** | She loves to read. |

> [!warning]
> Do not use **любити** when you just mean a mild preference. Use **подобається** for aesthetic appeal or mild likes. Мені подобається цей борщ. (I like this borsch.) Reserve **люблю** for strong affection or deep, active enjoyment! Я люблю читати хороші книги. (I love to read good books.)

## Я хочу (I want)

Що ви хочете робити? (What do you want to do?) Whether you are sharing your plans, expressing a desire, or simply ordering food, the дієслово **хотіти** (to want) is essential. Ми хочемо піти. (We want to go.) Вони хочуть їсти. (They want to eat.) Я хочу читати цей фільм. (I want to read this movie - wait, read a book!) Я хочу читати цю книгу. (I want to read this book. - Wait, 'цю книгу' is accusative!) Я хочу читати. (I want to read.) 

Expressing desires and wants is very straightforward in Ukrainian. We use the irregular дієслово **хотіти** (to want). Ми хочемо їсти смачний борщ. (We want to eat tasty borsch.)

Here is how the verb changes in the present tense:

| Pronoun | Verb form |
|---------|-----------|
| **я** | **хочу** |
| **ти** | **хочеш** |
| **він/вона** | **хоче** |
| **ми** | **хочемо** |
| **ви** | **хочете** |
| **вони** | **хочуть** |

You can use **я хочу** directly with an infinitive to state an action you want to do. This is very useful for planning your day. Я хочу читати. (I want to read.) Вони хочуть піти. (They want to go.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Я хочу їсти.** | I want to eat. |
| **Я хочу читати.** | I want to read. |
| **Я хочу піти.** | I want to go. |
| **Вона хоче їсти.** | She wants to eat. |

You can also use **хотіти** with objects. For now, we will use words that do not change their form when they are the object. Ми хочемо смачний борщ. (We want a tasty borsch.) Він хоче кіно. (He wants cinema.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Він хоче цей цікавий фільм.** | He wants this interesting movie. |
| **Ми хочемо смачний борщ.** | We want a tasty borsch. |
| **Я хочу їсти цей борщ.** | I want to eat this borsch. |

> [!culture]
> In English, saying "I want borsch" can sometimes sound a bit demanding, and people often prefer "I would like." In Ukrainian, **Я хочу борщ** is completely standard, neutral, and perfectly polite for everyday situations. You can confidently use it when ordering! Я хочу цей смачний борщ. (I want this tasty borsch.)

## Порівняння (Comparing Likes)

Conversation is a two-way street. After sharing what you like, it's polite to ask the other person about their preferences. Тобі подобається ця кава? (Do you like this coffee?) Тобі подобається цей хороший фільм? (Do you like this good movie?)

Asking others about their preferences is a great way to start a conversation. You can ask directly or compare their likes with your own. Ми любимо говорити. (We love to talk.)

To ask someone what they like using the first construction, use **Тобі подобається...?** (informal). Тобі подобається музика? (Do you like music?)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Тобі подобається ця кава?** | Do you like this coffee? |
| **Тобі подобається цей фільм?** | Do you like this movie? |

If you want to ask about strong preferences or active enjoyment, use **ти любиш** or **ви любите**. Ти любиш читати книги? (Do you love to read books?)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Ти любиш читати?** | Do you love to read? |
| **Ви любите смачний борщ?** | Do you love tasty borsch? |

To contrast your preference with someone else's, use the little word **а** (and/but). It is perfect for bouncing the question back and keeping the conversation going. Мені подобається кава. А тобі? (I like coffee. And you?) Я люблю кіно. А ти? (I love cinema. And you?)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Мені подобається кава. А тобі?** | I like coffee. And you? |
| **Я люблю цікавий фільм. А ти?** | I love an interesting movie. And you? |
| **Я хочу їсти. А ви хочете?** | I want to eat. And do you want to? |

If you strongly dislike something, you can describe it as boring. Це дуже нудний фільм. (This is a very boring movie.) Мені не подобається цей нудний фільм. (I do not like this boring movie.)

| Ukrainian | English |
|-----------|---------|
| **Це нудний фільм. Мені не подобається.** | This is a boring movie. I don't like it. |
| **Ця музика нудна.** | This music is boring. |

## Підсумок (Summary)

You now have three powerful verbs to express yourself in everyday Ukrainian. Let's review their core differences and grammatical patterns. Ми любимо українську мову. (We love the Ukrainian language.)

Let's review the three main ways to express your preferences and desires in Ukrainian. Each verb has its own specific grammar pattern and meaning. Це дуже цікаво. (This is very interesting.)

1. **Мені подобається** (it appeals to me) — used for mild likes and aesthetic appeal. The thing you like is the actual subject of the sentence. Example: *Мені подобається ця цікава музика.*
2. **Я люблю** (I love/enjoy) — used for strong affection or active enjoyment. You are the main subject. Example: *Я люблю читати хороші книги.*
3. **Я хочу** (I want) — used for desires and intentions. Followed by an infinitive or an object. Example: *Я хочу їсти смачний борщ.*

Check your understanding with these questions:

1. Яке дієслово ми використовуємо для сильної емоції? (Which verb do we use for a strong emotion?) — **любити**
2. Що ми використовуємо після слова "хочу", коли говоримо про дію? (What do we use after the word "want" when talking about an action?) — **інфінітив (infinitive)**
3. Як сказати "I like" українською? (How to say "I like" in Ukrainian?) — **мені подобається**
4. Як запитати "And you?" коли говоримо про "мені подобається"? (How to ask "And you?" when talking about "мені подобається"?) — **А тобі?**

    - statement: 'When using "Я хочу" to state an action, follow it with an infinitive.'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'Yes, you use the infinitive form, like "Я хочу їсти".'
    - statement: 'The verb "подобається" matches the object you like, not you.'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'In this construction, the object you like is the grammatical subject.'

      correct: false
      explanation: 'It must be "Мені подобається" (it is pleasing to me).'

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, the tool expects to find and replace exactly ONE occurrence of `old_string`. If you want to replace multiple occurrences of the exact same string, set `allow_multiple` to true. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the instance(s) to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations and `allow_multiple` is not true, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `allow_multiple` to true if you want to replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly.

- type: true-false
  title: 'Grammar Facts'
  instruction: 'Determine if the statement is true or false.'
  items:
    - statement: 'Use "Мені подобається" for strong, active enjoyment like "I love".'
      correct: false
      explanation: 'Use "Я люблю" for strong love. "Мені подобається" is for milder aesthetic appeal.'
    - statement: 'When using "Я хочу" to state an action, follow it with an infinitive.'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'Yes, you use the infinitive form, like "Я хочу їсти".'
    - statement: 'The verb "подобається" matches the object you like, not you.'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'In this construction, the object you like is the grammatical subject.'
    - statement: 'We say "Я подобається" to mean "I like".'
      correct: false
      explanation: 'It must be "Мені подобається" (it is pleasing to me).'
    - statement: 'To ask a friend what they like, use "Тобі подобається?".'
      correct: true
      explanation: '"Тобі" is the "to you" form of "ти".'
    - statement: 'The word "хочу" is the "I" form of the verb to want.'
      correct: true
      explanation: '"Я хочу" means "I want".'
    - statement: 'We use "подобаються" when we like only one thing.'
      correct: false
      explanation: '"Подобаються" is for plural things, like flowers (квіти).'
    - statement: 'You can say "Я хочу читати" to mean "I want to read".'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'You can use an infinitive verb after "хочу".'

- type: true-false
  title: 'Grammar Facts'
  instruction: 'Determine if the statement is true or false.'
  items:
    - statement: 'Use "Мені подобається" for strong, active enjoyment like "I love".'
      correct: false
      explanation: 'Use "Я люблю" for strong love. "Мені подобається" is for milder aesthetic appeal.'
    - statement: 'When using "Я хочу" to state an action, follow it with an infinitive.'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'Yes, you use the infinitive form, like "Я хочу їсти".'
    - statement: 'The verb "подобається" matches the object you like, not you.'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'In this construction, the object you like is the grammatical subject.'
    - statement: 'We say "Я подобається" to mean "I like".'
      correct: false
      explanation: 'It must be "Мені подобається" (it is pleasing to me).'
    - statement: 'To ask a friend what they like, use "Тобі подобається?".'
      correct: true
      explanation: '"Тобі" is the "to you" form of "ти".'
    - statement: 'The word "хочу" is the "I" form of the verb to want.'
      correct: true
      explanation: '"Я хочу" means "I want".'
    - statement: 'We use "подобаються" when we like only one thing.'
      correct: false
      explanation: '"Подобаються" is for plural things, like flowers (квіти).'
    - statement: 'You can say "Я хочу читати" to mean "I want to read".'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'You can use an infinitive verb after "хочу".'

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, the tool expects to find and replace exactly ONE occurrence of `old_string`. If you want to replace multiple occurrences of the exact same string, set `allow_multiple` to true. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the instance(s) to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations and `allow_multiple` is not true, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `allow_multiple` to true if you want to replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly.

toolCalls.name: replace

- type: true-false
  title: 'Grammar Facts'
  instruction: 'Determine if the statement is true or false.'
  items:
    - statement: 'Use "Мені подобається" for strong, active enjoyment like "I love".'
      correct: false
      explanation: 'Use "Я люблю" for strong love. "Мені подобається" is for milder aesthetic appeal.'
    - statement: 'When using "Я хочу" to state an action, follow it with an infinitive.'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'Yes, you use the infinitive form, like "Я хочу їсти".'
    - statement: 'The verb "подобається" matches the object you like, not you.'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'In this construction, the object you like is the grammatical subject.'
    - statement: 'We say "Я подобається" to mean "I like".'
      correct: false
      explanation: 'It must be "Мені подобається" (it is pleasing to me).'
    - statement: 'To ask a friend what they like, use "Тобі подобається?".'
      correct: true
      explanation: '"Тобі" is the "to you" form of "ти".'
    - statement: 'The word "хочу" is the "I" form of the verb to want.'
      correct: true
      explanation: '"Я хочу" means "I want".'
    - statement: 'We use "подобаються" when we like only one thing.'
      correct: false
      explanation: '"Подобаються" is for plural things, like flowers (квіти).'
    - statement: 'You can say "Я хочу читати" to mean "I want to read".'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'You can use an infinitive verb after "хочу".'

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, the tool expects to find and replace exactly ONE occurrence of `old_string`. If you want to replace multiple occurrences of the exact same string, set `allow_multiple` to true. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the instance(s) to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations and `allow_multiple` is not true, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `allow_multiple` to true if you want to replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly.

toolCalls.name: replace

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, the tool expects to find and replace exactly ONE occurrence of `old_string`. If you want to replace multiple occurrences of the exact same string, set `allow_multiple` to true. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the instance(s) to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations and `allow_multiple` is not true, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `allow_multiple` to true if you want to replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly.

toolCalls.name: replace

- type: true-false
  title: 'Grammar Facts'
  instruction: 'Determine if the statement is true or false.'
  items:
    - statement: 'Use "Мені подобається" for strong, active enjoyment like "I love".'
      correct: false
      explanation: 'Use "Я люблю" for strong love. "Мені подобається" is for milder aesthetic appeal.'
    - statement: 'When using "Я хочу" to state an action, follow it with an infinitive.'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'Yes, you use the infinitive form, like "Я хочу їсти".'
    - statement: 'The verb "подобається" matches the object you like, not you.'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'In this construction, the object you like is the grammatical subject.'
    - statement: 'We say "Я подобається" to mean "I like".'
      correct: false
      explanation: 'It must be "Мені подобається" (it is pleasing to me).'
    - statement: 'To ask a friend what they like, use "Тобі подобається?".'
      correct: true
      explanation: '"Тобі" is the "to you" form of "ти".'
    - statement: 'The word "хочу" is the "I" form of the verb to want.'
      correct: true
      explanation: '"Я хочу" means "I want".'
    - statement: 'We use "подобаються" when we like only one thing.'
      correct: false
      explanation: '"Подобаються" is for plural things, like flowers (квіти).'
    - statement: 'You can say "Я хочу читати" to mean "I want to read".'
      correct: true
      explanation: 'You can use an infinitive verb after "хочу".'

toolCalls.description:
Replaces text within a file. By default, the tool expects to find and replace exactly ONE occurrence of `old_string`. If you want to replace multiple occurrences of the exact same string, set `allow_multiple` to true. This tool requires providing significant context around the change to ensure precise targeting. Always use the read_file tool to examine the file's current content before attempting a text replacement.
      
      The user has the ability to modify the `new_string` content. If modified, this will be stated in the response.
      
      Expectation for required parameters:
      1. `old_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace (including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.).
      2. `new_string` MUST be the exact literal text to replace `old_string` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic and that `old_string` and `new_string` are different.
      3. `instruction` is the detailed instruction of what needs to be changed. It is important to Make it specific and detailed so developers or large language models can understand what needs to be changed and perform the changes on their own if necessary. 
      4. NEVER escape `old_string` or `new_string`, that would break the exact literal text requirement.
      **Important:** If ANY of the above are not satisfied, the tool will fail. CRITICAL for `old_string`: Must uniquely identify the instance(s) to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations and `allow_multiple` is not true, the tool will fail.
      5. Prefer to break down complex and long changes into multiple smaller atomic calls to this tool. Always check the content of the file after changes or not finding a string to match.
      **Multiple replacements:** Set `allow_multiple` to true if you want to replace ALL occurrences that match `old_string` exactly.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.
